Public reports do not expose a uniform cost metric for interactive world models. We therefore compare reported training-video scale where direct hour-level figures are available from primary sources. [10, 2].

Refer to caption

## 10 Release Status and Next Steps

DF-World 0.1 Preview is a preview, not a full model release. DreamForge does not currently open-source the preview checkpoint. DF-World 0.5 is planned as a more complete model generation and may be released publicly if licensing, safety, and engineering constraints allow. [...] ##### Report GitHub Issue

results.title: DreamForge Review: Can AI Really Build You a Custom Video Game From Scratch? | Yardbarker
results.content:
## The Good

DreamForge is very easy to use. Since it uses LLMs as the basis, chatting with Forge is simple and intuitive. I was impressed with how quickly it generated the final game. That was some impressive processing power. I’ve used LLMs for generating content, and it isn’t always fast. The comic-style panels were a good way to transition between scenes. And they make great screenshots. The opening theme song was a surprising touch, even if some of the lyrics didn’t make a lot of sense. I was amused and had fun playing with it.

## Needs Improvement

I’ll caveat this by saying I know this is early access and the platform has a long way to go. But we’re judging it as it stands now. [...] ## How it Works

After seeing DreamForge in action, I wondered what was behind it all. You direct an animated avatar, and the other characters move about. You talk to them in natural language. And each has their own voice, plus a narrator. There are AI-generated images, comic-book-like scenes, and that theme song. That really took me by surprise and made me laugh. AI does some silly things. [...] Finally, I was presented with the game, which had a load screen, theme song (with lyrics), and fully voiced characters. The game was simple point-and-click mechanics. It’s basically going through an interactive story where you talk to characters (by typing to them), manipulate objects, and make choices.

Altogether, it was just over half an hour from the start to completing a playthrough of my creation. If you have access to DreamForge yourself, you can play it by clicking here.

## How it Works

results.title: I'm Tired of BROKEN Video Game Releases
results.content: micro stuttering, long shader load times. Oh my god, those shader times are horrible, and even crashes during gameplay. In fact, some frustrated users even claimed that the game runs way worse than it did in the open beta. And that's very, very embarrassing. And Capcom clearly knew the PC performance was bad because they've practically tried to hide it under fancy new tech. The very first time you launched a game, it immediately suggests you should enable frame generation if you have something like an Nvidia 40 series card or an AMD RDNA3 or RX7000 series GPU. You know, the AI interpolation that adds fake frames. And you know, this has been marketed as a PC performance booster or a FPS performance booster, but it's really just more of a smoothing trick that can make your game appear [...] just wasn't ready for prime time. And Minds Eye is just one example. We've seen other AAA releases recently come out in rough shape, then scramble with patches while their reputation just tanks. The pattern is exhausting. As consumers were tired of spending $70 or actually now $80 plus on a new game to only act as beta testers for the first few months with Monster Hunter Wild, it's especially disappointing because Capcom knows how to make good games. They've had several great game of the year contenders over the year and that, you know, attributes to their solid track record. That's partly why this situation is just so frustrating. Whether it was rushing to meet a fiscal quarter deadline or just underestimating the work that was needed for the PC launch, the result that squandered an [...] more of a smoothing trick that can make your game appear smoother, but it adds quite a bit of latency on top and can also introduce weird artifacting or shimmering. And on top of that, you also have resolution upscaling such as, you know, DLSS um or AMD's FSR. And those are actually turned on by default in all of the presets. That's right. The game isn't even rendering at native resolution out of the box. It's literally lowering the internal resolution and then upscaling it through AI upscaling to hide the poor frame rates. It just tries to shove it down your throat because it's enabled in all of the default presets. And that is a terrible look. I like frame generation and I think stuff like DLSS and FSR are great as optional features, especially when you have older hardware, but here it

results.title: New World review | PC Gamer
results.content:
But even during these large-scale scraps there are frustrations. The fights are an absolute clusterfuck with this many players, so you just jump in and hope for the best, but the game might decide that, actually, there are too many players trying to have fun right now. Half of my attempts to fight in forts have met with failure because there's a limit to the number of players not just inside a fort, but around it. If you charge in, you're given 10 seconds to leave the zone or you'll be unceremoniously teleported all the way back to the nearest settlement.

Sitting in bed

Sitting in bed [...] With its beefy crafting system, open PvP, player-led wars and dynamic economy, it does so much right on paper, but the reality is a lot less scintillating: hour after hour of running through forests you've long grown sick of seeing, facing the same enemies over and over for most of those 60 levels, praying for any kind of novelty to liberate the experience from the doldrums.

## Identity crisis [...] you're just getting the odd passive bonus and not much else.
